coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for label-free detection of physiological potassium level in human serum and red blood cells
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Lingyang
Chen, Shenna
Zhao, Qian
Huang, Haowen
coredata.dc:description: A unique photoluminescence carbon dots (CDs) with larger size were prepared by microwave-assisted method. Complex functional groups on the surface of the CDs facilitate the nanoparticles to form affinity with some metal ions. Taking advantage of the effective fluorescence quenching effect of K+, a highly sensitive CD-based fluorescence analytical system for label-free detection of K+ with limit of detection (LOD) 1.0×10−12 M was established. The concentrations of potassium ion in biological samples such as human serum are usually found at millimolar levels or even higher. The proposed method begins with a substantial dilution of the sample to place the K+ concentration in the dynamic range for quantification, which covers 3 orders of magnitude. This offers some advantages: the detection of K+ only needs very small quantities of biological samples, and the dilution of samples such as serum may effectively eliminate the potential interferences that often originate from the background matrix. The determined potassium levels were satisfactory and closely comparable with the results given by the hospital, indicating that this fluorescent probe is applicable to detection of physiological potassium level with high accuracy. Compared with other relative biosensors requiring modified design, bio-molecular modification or/and sophisticated instruments, this CD-based sensor is very simple, cost-effective and easy detection, suggesting great potential applications for successively monitoring physiological potassium level and the change in biological system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Potassium ion
Label free
Human serum
Human red blood cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of potassium ion in human serum and red blood cell (RBC) samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of detection of physiological potassium level in human serum and red blood cells based on fluorescent CDs.

UV–vis absorption (black line), excited and emission spectra (red lines) of the CDs, inset: photographs of CDs under ambient daylight (left) and UV light (right). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescent intensity of CDs versus pH.
TEM image (a), size distribution (b), the XRD patterns (c) and FT-IR spectrum (d) of the as-prepared CDs.

Selective detection of K+ using CDs as fluorescent probes. The concentration of all the metal ions is 10−4 M. F 0 represents the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared CDs and F is the intensity after the addition of EDTA to the mixture solution of CDs and metal ion. λ ex =350nm.

Fluorescence quenching of CDs with varying amounts of K+ (K+ concentration is increasing from top to bottom). Inset: a linear relationship of ΔF versus the logarithm of concentrations of K+ over the range from 1×10−10 to 5×10−8 M. λ ex =350nm. The error bar represents the standard deviation of five measurements.
Determination of blood potassium in human serum samples.

row.entry: K+ found by indirect ion-selective electrodes (mM)
K+ found by the proposed CDs sensor (mM)

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for label-free detection of physiological potassium level in human serum and red blood cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Hunan Provincial University Key Laboratory of QSAR/QSPR, Key Laboratory of Theoretical Organic Chemistry and Function Molecule, Ministry of Education, Xiangtan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Hunan University of Science and Technology
Hunan Provincial University Key Laboratory of QSAR/QSPR
Key Laboratory of Theoretical Organic Chemistry and Function Molecule

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A unique photoluminescence carbon dots (CDs) with larger size were prepared by microwave-assisted method. Complex functional groups on the surface of the CDs facilitate the nanoparticles to form affinity with some metal ions. Taking advantage of the effective fluorescence quenching effect of K+, a highly sensitive CD-based fluorescence analytical system for label-free detection of K+ with limit of detection (LOD) 1.0×10−12 M was established. The concentrations of potassium ion in biological samples such as human serum are usually found at millimolar levels or even higher. The proposed method begins with a substantial dilution of the sample to place the K+ concentration in the dynamic range for quantification, which covers 3 orders of magnitude. This offers some advantages: the detection of K+ only needs very small quantities of biological samples, and the dilution of samples such as serum may effectively eliminate the potential interferences that often originate from the background matrix. The determined potassium levels were satisfactory and closely comparable with the results given by the hospital, indicating that this fluorescent probe is applicable to detection of physiological potassium level with high accuracy. Compared with other relative biosensors requiring modified design, bio-molecular modification or/and sophisticated instruments, this CD-based sensor is very simple, cost-effective and easy detection, suggesting great potential applications for successively monitoring physiological potassium level and the change in biological system.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nonmaterials have attracted much attention due to their unique properties. Owing to the robust chemical inertness, size dependent photoluminescence, high photostability against photo bleaching and blinking, low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, carbon dots (CDs) have been successfully utilized in bio-imaging, sensors, photocatalysis and optoelectronic devices [1–6]. As new class of fluorescent materials, CDs and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been extensively studied as sensing probes for monitoring many materials such as thrombin, glucose, Hg2+, Cu2+ and Fe3+ [7–12]. Recently, CDs have been actively pursued and applied in biosensing [13], which greatly facilitate the sensing application of CDs and provoke further efforts to develop their bio-analytical applications.

Potassium ions play key roles in biological systems, such as nerve transmission, maintenance of muscular strength and extracellular osmolarity, enzyme activation, regulation of blood pressure [14–18]. Abnormal K+ concentration is a symptom of several diseases, including kidney diseases, alcoholism, anorexia, bulimia, heart disease, diabetes and cancer [19]. Thus, selective and sensitive K+ sensors are important for the detection of physiological potassium level to facilitate diagnosis and treatment. A variety of analytical methods for K+ determination have been developed, including atomic absorption spectrophotometry [20,21], electrochemical method [22,23], spectrophotometry [24], atomic emission spectrometry [25], ion chromatography [26,27] and so on. Most of them, however, require complicated instrumentation and involve cumbersome laboratory procedures, which limit the scope of their practical application. Some of them also suffer from disadvantages such as relatively high detection limits and narrow detection ranges. Among the biologically important cations, accurate analysis of K+ is more difficult because there are many conditions involving variations in the electrolyte content of the body fluids. One of the difficulties in monitoring K+ under physiological conditions comes from the coexistence of Na+. It is therefore of great significance to explore a rapid analytical assay for detection of K+ in biological systems. Therefore, a simple and sensitive alternative without using complex instruments is highly desired Scheme 1 .

In this study, we prepared unique photoluminescence CDs with larger size using facile and green one-step microwave-assisted method. The potassium ion can effectively quench the fluorescence of the unmodified CDs, and a selective CDs-based fluorescence analytical system for label-free detection of K+ was therefore established. The unique CDs may be used as fluorescent probe to sensitively detect physiological potassium level in human serum without any pretreatment. The results were almost in agreement with those obtained by the currently used ion-selective electrode method in clinical diagnosis. Furthermore, the physiological potassium level of human red blood cells (RBC) was determined by this proposed method.

d-Glucose, potassium chloride, aluminum sulfate, silver nitrate, zinc nitrate, calcium nitrate, ferric chloride, manganese sulfate, copper nitrate, cobalt sulfate, nickel sulfate, sodium chloride, cadmium nitrate, ammonia chloride, plumbous nitrate, magnesium chloride, mercuric chloride were purchased from Sinopharm Chmical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All of the chemicals, unless mentioned otherwise, were of analytical reagent grade and used as received. The aqueous solutions were prepared in doubly distilled water.

The CDs were synthesized by a microwave-assisted method as bellow: 5g glucose was dissolved in 75mL of doubly distilled water with sonication to form solution. Then the solution was heated in a domestic microwave for 20min. The obtained product was centrifuged (16,000× g, 30min). After that, the resultant supernate containing luminescent CDs was dialyzed with a dialysis tube (molecular weight 1000) against doubly distilled water for 24h, and finally the product was obtained.

3.00mL of CDs was placed in a quartz cell of 10mm path length. Then, a certain amount of K+ was added to the cell at room temperature and the corresponding fluorescence spectrum was recorded after 15min. For comparison, different metal ions such as Mg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Na+, Ag+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Hg2+ were added to 3.00mL of CDs with the final concentration of 10−4 M, and incubated for 15min at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation at 360nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of potassium ion in human serum and red blood cell (RBC) samples
ce:section.ce:para: A series of standard potassium solution (1.0×10−8, 5.0×10−8, 1.0×10−7, 5.0×10−7, 1.0×10−6, 5.0×10−6 M) were chosen to establish calibration curve for the subsequently quantitative detection of K+. 30μL of different concentrations of potassium solution was added to 3mL of CDs solution, and the corresponding fluorescent spectra were recorded.

All the serum samples were obtained from the hospital. The serum samples were stored at 4°C until use while the whole vein blood samples (2mL each) were separately put into heparinized containers, diluted with 5mL of phosphate buffer saline (PBS) containing 0.9% NaCl, and centrifuged at 2500rpm for 5min. After washing three times with PBS, the cells were re-suspended in PBS. The size distributions of the RBCs was measured on the Sysmex XE 5000 (Sysmex Corporation, Kobe, Japan), a fully automated blood cell counter on which the RBCs were detected at the center of the aperture employing a Sheath Flow DC detection method. In the process of the experiments, the serum was first diluted 10,000 times, then, 30μL of diluted human serum sample without any pretreatment was added in 3mL of CDs PBS buffer solution, and the fluorescent spectra were recorded subsequently.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a Tecnai G2 20 (USA) operating at 100kV. For TEM sample preparation, 1–2μL of the CDs solution was placed on a carbon coated copper grid and allowed to dry at room temperature. UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs were measured using Lambda 35 (PerkinElmer, USA) and the fluorescence intensity spectra were recorded by a RF-5301PC (Shimadzu, Japan) fluorescence spectrometer.

To evaluate the optical properties of CDs, the UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra were investigated. Fig. 1 shows the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the water-soluble CDs. It can be seen that there are two absorbance bands centered at around 223, 282nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. Similar to the previous reports [28,29], the peak at 223nm should be ascribed to the π–π∗ transitions of the aromatic CC sp2 domains which cannot produce observed fluorescence signal. However, another absorption peak centered at 282nm arises from the trapping of excited state energy of the surface states, which results in strong fluorescence [30]. When excited at 350nm, the CDs exhibited strong PL emission centered at 450nm. From the photograph shown in Fig. 1 inset, the diluted CDs aqueous solution is brown yellow under ambient daylight but exhibits a very intense blue color when irradiate with a UV light (365nm), indicating the blue fluorescent property of the CDs. The strong fluorescence of the CDs may result from the emissive traps of the surface [31–33]. Moreover, the fluorescent variation of CDs with different pH was investigated. The pH of the solution had a great effect on the fluorescence of CDs, as shown in Fig. 2 . The fluorescence intensity decreased along the increased pH in the range from 7.0 to 13.6, which may be related to the presence of the group COO− on the surface of CDs. As observed from other carbon-based quantum dots [34–37], the fluorescence of the present CDs also changes with the excitation wavelength.

Fig. 3 a and b shows the typical TEM image and the size distribution of as-prepared CDs, revealing that these CDs were spherical with a diameter of ∼20nm. The XRD patterns of the CDs (Fig. 3c) display two broad peaks centered at 2θ =18° and 37°, which are attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms, similar to the previous report [38]. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3d) may indicate the surface functional groups of the synthesized CDs. The peaks at 3414cm−1 correspond to the stretching mode of OH, while the peaks at 1635cm−1 and 1416cm−1 illustrate the existence of COO−. The peak 1026cm−1 shows the group CCH. All of the above FTIR results demonstrate that the surfaces of CDs are coated with hydrophilic groups (hydroxyl and carboxylic groups), which suggest the excellent aqueous solubility.

The FTIR spectrum indicates that there are various oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs, which might interact with metal ions. To explore the CDs response to metal ions, the influence of different metal ions upon the fluorescence of CDs was investigated. First, various biologically and environmentally relevant metal ions, including K+, Na+, Ag+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Na+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+ and Al3+ ions were added into the CDs solution, and the fluorescence responses were recorded. As shown in Fig. 4 , significant fluorescence quenching effect was observed with the addition of K+, Fe3+, Co2+, Cu2+ and Ni2+, while others metal ions showed only a slight quenching effect and the influence could be almost negligible. We then tested whether the as-prepared CDs might be used to fabricate a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence sensor for K+ without further modification. As demonstrated in Fig. 4, after the addition of EDTA, the system including CDs and metal ions exhibits a remarkable change of fluorescence intensity. Due to the introduction of EDTA into these ions to form complexes, only the K+ can quench the fluorescent CDs. Furthermore, anions such as SO4 2−, NO3 −, Cl− and PO4 3− did not affect the fluorescence of CDs. Therefore, the CDs prepared here could be used as a potential label-free fluorescent probe to detect K+ ions in an aquatic system.

As demonstrated by the earlier report, the surface states of CDs can be influenced by metal ions [39]. The functional groups tethered on the surface of CDs may affect the properties. As a result, the fluorescent CDs may be quenched by some metal ions. In this study, groups of OH and COO− on the surface of the as-prepared CDs may facilitate to bind some metal ions to the surface of the CDs, such as Fe3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ni2+ and K+, then change the surface state of CDs, exhibiting a strong effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. With the addition of the chelate EDTA to the system including CDs and these metal ions, the quenched fluorescence may quickly recover to the initial intensity with the exception of K+ due to the formation of coordinate bond between the metal ions (exception of K+) and EDTA. To the best of our knowledge, no research work on fluorescent CDs responsible to K+ was reported. In this research, additional three kinds of CDs were prepared and a comparison was performed. CDs prepared by hydrothermal method from glucose show strong fluorescence intensity, however, no appreciable fluorescent response to K+ was found. With the change of source composed of glucose and cysteine or PEG200, the prepared fluorescent CDs show slight response to K+. The TEM images shown in Fig. S1 (Supplementary information) indicate that they have similar morphology and size (∼2nm), obviously smaller than the above CDs prepared by microwave-assisted method. Fig. S2 shows the comparison of their FTIR spectra. From the TEM images and FTIR spectra, the different size of CDs and slight different functional group coated on the surface of CDs illustrate they have different surface state, resulting in the unique CDs clearly responsible to K+.

The groups on the surface of CDs terminated with OH and COO− may form hydrogen bonding leading to the formation of cyclic structure. The interesting feature of this cyclic structure around the CDs is the presence of cavity that may accommodate selectivity of potassium cation, similar to the crown ethers, which are an important variety of host macrocyclic molecules exhibiting sensitive and selective binding properties to metal cations and neutral molecules [40,41]. After the inclusion of K+ into these cavities, strong interactions between the K+ and the lone pairs of the oxygen donor atoms might lead to more stable structure, providing a rationale for the selectively stability imparted by K+ binding to these structures, thus, result in the quench of fluorescent CDs. Such assay selectivity is comparable to that of the expensive double-labeled G-quadruplex oligonucleotide based method [42,43]. The other two kinds of CDs prepared by hydrothermal method with smaller size (∼2nm) have almost the same groups on the surface and also may form cyclic structure by hydrogen bond between the OH and COO− groups. Compared to the larger size CDs with 20nm, however, these cavities are too small to impart the K+, and these CDs exhibit no fluorescent response to K+.

Under the same conditions, we evaluated the capability of this fluorescent probe for quantitative detection of K+. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 450nm decreased gradually with increasing concentration of K+. In addition, the intrinsic spectral peak of CD-K+ was unchanged in comparison with that of free CDs. Fig. 5b presents the fluorescence data versus the concentration of K+ ions. A good linear correlation (R 2 =0.9954) is obtained over the concentration range of 1×10−10 to 5×10−8 M and the limit of detection (LOD) as low as 1×10−12 M is obtained (S/N=3), indicating this method is of very high sensitivity. In these experiments, five parallel experiments were carried out and the relative standard deviation was 1.27%, indicating this CD-based sensor with high accuracy.

The excellent specificity combined with high sensitivity and fast response of CDs to K+ suggests that this sensor might be directly applied to the detection of K+ in real samples. By using this fluorescent CDs probe, the human serum samples were analyzed to demonstrate the practical application from volunteers. The human serum is very complex. Before the determination of K+ level in serum, the influence of higher level of Na+ and background of human serum on the determination of K+ was explored, as shown in Fig. S3. The results indicate that no interference of the higher level of Na+ and background of human serum on the detection of K+ is found. The potassium ion levels of fresh serum samples were first determined in the local hospital with indirect ion-selective electrode. The proposed method begins with a substantial dilution of the sample to place the K+ concentration in the dynamic range for quantification, which covers 3 orders of magnitude. The spectra of the fluorescence quenching of CDs with varying amounts serum samples were collected, as shown in Fig. S4. As a result, the blood potassium levels are determined and displayed in Table 1 , which were satisfactory and closely comparable with the results given by the hospital. This confirms that the proposed sensing system is applicable to blood potassium analysis with high accuracy. In this developed sensor, the detection of K+ only needs very small quantities of biological samples, and the dilution of serum sample may effectively eliminate the potential interferences that often originate from the background matrix.

Another advantage of this highly sensitive fluorescent probe to K+ is that only a very small sample volume in one measurement. It is thus suitable for the detection of trace amounts of K+ in other biological sample such as RBCs. Following the general performance, the potassium level of RBCs was also measured. To ensure precise determination, the cells after collection were immediately separated from the blood sample by centrifugation. In order to ensure all the bio-potassium ions to react completely with the CDs, the RBCs were ultrasonically ruptured, then, the resulting ruptured RBCs were added to the CDs solution. Subsequently, the fluorescence quenching of the CDs was recorded at different time intervals such as 5, 30 and 120min. The potassium level of the RBCs was thus determined by measuring the quenched fluorescence from samples containing 1256, 503, and 251 cells (Fig. S5). The data are quite close with an average concentration at 97.57mM, which again agrees with reported data [44,45]. According to the average volume of RBCs (82–96×10−15 L [46]), the potassium level in a single RBC may be calculated to be 3.4×10−13 g and this result coincides with the previous study [47].

In summary, we prepared a kind of fluorescent CDs with facile and green one-step microwave-assisted method. Taking advantage of the effective fluorescence quenching effect of K+, a selective CD-based fluorescence analytical system for label-free detection of K+ was established. A comparison between the unique CDs with larger size and other CDs indicates that a slight difference of functional groups on the surface of the CDs would lead to this fluorescent probe possessing highly efficient and sensitive chemosensor for selective detection of K+ with LOD 1.0×10−12 M. Moreover, the proposed CD-based sensor exhibits promising application to directly and rapidly detect physiological potassium level of human serum. The determined levels of potassium were closely comparable with the results given by the hospital, showing that this fluorescent probe is applicable to the detection of physiological potassium level with high accuracy. The proposed method offers some advantages: the detection of K+ only needs very small quantities of biological samples, and the dilution of samples such as serum may effectively eliminate the potential interferences that often originate from the background matrix. Compared with other relative biosensors requiring modified design, bio-molecular modification or/and sophisticated instruments, this CD-based sensor is very simple, cost-effective and easy detection, suggesting great potential applications for successively monitoring physiological potassium level and the change in biological system.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21375036), Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (14JJ7054), the Innovation Platform Open Funds for Universities in Hunan Province (13K090).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

Layer-by-layer immobilization of carbon dots fluorescent nanomaterials on single optical fiber
Synthesis-modification integration: one-step fabrication of boronic acid functionalized carbon dots for fluorescent blood sugar sensing
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a turn-off fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions
Carbon dots based dual-emission silica nanoparticles as a ratiometric nanosensor for Cu2+
Synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for sensitive, label-free detection of Fe(III) in aqueous media
Carbon-dot-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detecting hydrogen sulfide in aqueous media and inside live cells
The quantification of potassium using a quartz crystal microbalance
A fluorescent sensor with high selectivity and sensitivity for potassium in water
A defect in the Kv channel-interacting protein 2 (KChIP2) gene leads to a complete loss of I to and confers susceptibility to ventricular tachycardia
Role of astrocytes in the clearance of excess extracellular potassium
A new highly selective fluorescent K+ sensor
Selective spectrophotometric determination of trinitrotoluene trinitrophenol, dinitrophenol and mononitrophenol
The use of microemulsion for determination of sodium and potassium in biodiesel by flame atomic absorption spectrometry
Sensitive detection of potassium ion using Prussian blue nanotube sensor
A robust electronic switch made of immobilized duplex/quadruplex DNA
The determination of potassium concentration in vitreous humor by low pressure ion chromatography and its application in the estimation of postmortem interval
Determination of sodium and potassium in biodiesel by flame atomic emission spectrometry, with dissolution in ethanol as a single sample preparation step
Development of a potassium ion-selective fluorescent sensor based on 3-styrylated BODIPY
A novel label-free fluorescent sensor for the detection of potassium ion based on DNAzyme
Graphene quantum dot as a green and facile sensor for free chlorine in drinking water
Electrochemical tuning of luminescent carbon nanodots: from preparation to luminescence mechanism
Toward quantitatively fluorescent carbon-based quantum dots
Bandgap-like strong fluorescence in functionalized carbon nanoparticles
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
A green and facile approach for the synthesis of water soluble fluorescent carbon dots from banana juice
Carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal treatment of dopamine as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for the label-free detection of iron(III) ions and dopamine
Deep ultraviolet photoluminescence of water-soluble self-passivated graphene quantum dots
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning sensors, and bioimaging
A biocompatible fluorescent ink based on water-soluble luminescent carbon nanodots
Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
Effect of molecular vibrations on the selectivity character of pyridino-18-crown-6 derivatives towards potassium ion
Sensing metal ions with ion selectivity of a crown ether and fluorescence resonance energy transfer between carbon dots and grapheme
Telomeric DNA dimerizes by formation of guanine tetrads between hairpin loops
A pyrene-labeled G-quadruplex oligonucleotide as a fluorescent probe for potassium ion detection in biological applications
The sodium, potassium, and water contents of red blood cells of healthy human adults
Determination of potassium in red blood cells using unmeasured volumes of whole blood and combined sodium/potassium-selective membrane electrode measurements
Estimation of red blood cell volume fraction from overall permittivity measurements
Detection of sodium and potassium in single human red blood cells by 193-nm laser ablative sampling: a feasibility demonstration
